figures.caption: Fig. 14: Visual examples of our model running in autoregressive mode to generate long video sequences on the ScanNet++ dataset.
Fig. 15: Qualitative comparison on the ScanNet++ dataset between DINO-Control 2D and Wan2.2 under Canny edge and depth conditioning.

Fig. 9: We show two more applications of Control-DINO: (a) without lighting specific changes we are able to produce relighting results which are comparable to methods designed for the task [53]; (b) We can also perform domain transfer from simulated videos to photorealistic ones. The model has never seen sim/real pairs of driving videos but still succeeds in preserving the real appearance while being controlled by sim features.
Fig. 22: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (last 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "FastStyle Udnie".
Fig. 21: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (last 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "Drawing Contour Style".
Fig. 19: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (first 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "CartoonGAN Paprika".
Fig. 20: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (first 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "Cool Moonlight".

Table 4: Video-from-3D comparison on ScanNet++. We evaluate geometry-driven 3D-derived conditioning methods. We compare quantitative results for image-space baselines (Wan and 2D Control-DINO) and report results for 3D-derived methods.

Fig. 10: Overview of appearance augmentations used during training and validation. Training: We apply neural style transfer (AnimeGAN, CartoonGAN, FastStyle, Drawing) and photometric augmentations (color temperature, contrast, saturation shifts) to the training videos. Validation: We evaluate generalization using held-out styles including InstantStyle with famous paintings, unseen neural style transfer models, and IC-Light relighting with varying lighting conditions.
Fig. 18: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (first 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "Bright Daylight".
Fig. 17: We show here additional results on DL3DV140 to generate "real" sequences.
Fig. 8: Additional 2D transfer results on different scenes and different validation styles.

Fig. 7: We compare to related work on 2 scenes from DL3DV140 for reconstruction. Our method can faithfully regenerate the output with minor lighting and color variations while the other methods have significantly weaker semantic following and only preserve the approximate scene layout.

Fig. 4: Results for DINO stylization and dramatic light changes (GT Frame is in the bottom left of the image). Our method is able to strongly retain the original geometry and semantics while allowing for appearance and color to change.
Fig. 5: Visualisation of 3D structure used on ScanNet++ first frames. Top row: voxel RGB renderings. Bottom row: mesh video renderings.

Fig. 6: Qualitative comparison at matched time steps for four scenes of the ScanNet++ dataset with conditioning signal example. We show generated outputs at t=35 and t=49 for 2D, voxel, Concerto, mesh and 3DGS conditioning. The last row at t=0 shows the conditioning dino feature maps signal for each method, while the GT frame at t=0 is the common starting frame for all methods.
Fig. 16: Control-DINO applied to a dynamic video with moving pedestrians, demonstrating compatibility with neural style transfer (CartoonGAN) and photometric augmentation (Noir).
Fig. 12: Visual examples of augmentations to the conditioning signal: different voxel sizes(default 0.02), point clouds in place of voxels, and downscaled feature maps.

Fig. 13: Temporal stability of the generated content when looping the conditioning signal while running our method autoregressively. Frames with identical camera poses occur at t=1/98/196, t=25/74/123/172, and t=49/147, allowing comparison of scene consistency over long time horizons.
Fig. 11: PCA visualization of the high-resolution and true resolution conditioning feature maps (60×90).

Table 1: Quantitative comparison on T&T Appearance Augmented. All models are trained on DL3DV and evaluated on the T&T (for two frame sampling strides) with a combination of 15 different lighting or stylistic variations per scene different from the training data. Overall our method produces the most 3D consistent videos, especially for longer camera trajectory, while maintaining a competitive CLIP similarity with the first frame in the sequence. Our same model but trained only on real data performs much worse in style following.
Fig. 24: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (last 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "Van Gogh Starry Night".
Fig. 23: Control-DINO (first 3 columns) and edges (last 3 columns) conditioning results for Tanks and Temples transfer on "Sunset Left".

Fig. 3: (Left) During training, DINOv3 features from the original video condition the trainable Control-DINO branch, while the frozen backbone denoises appearanceaugmented latents of the same scene. (Right) At inference, conditioning features (2D or 3D-rendered) guide generation through Control-DINO. A transferred first frame sets the target appearance.
